one_line_takeaway: By inspecting only the anomaly statistics of a model's final classification layer (62 hand-designed indicators) rather than reverse-engineering triggers, DFBScanner detects backdoors in ~1 ms on CPU with 97.17% TPR / 0.95% FPR across a 5,124-model benchmark, but an attacker who freezes the final layer during backdoor injection can drop its TPR to as low as 2.5-11.7%.
key_finding: On the small benchmark (1,200 backdoor / 210 clean models), the best DFBScanner variant (DFBScanner-M, model-level indicator selection) achieves 97.17% TPR / 0.95% FPR with an average detection time of ~1.06 ms per model on CPU, versus baselines: NC 45.33% TPR / 13.81% FPR (no reported time on TinyImageNet, listed as >24h), FeatureRE 43.67%/12.86% (13,402 s total), DF-TND 22.42%/10.48% (9,970 s), DQ 64.08%/11.43% (0.23 ms -- fastest prior method but unstable, e.g. it fails entirely on some architecture-dataset pairs), FreeEagle 37.92%/12.38% (597.9 s), MM-BD 38.67%/21.43% (456.4 s), and BARBIE 87.85% TPR but a very high 59.05% FPR (6,107.7 s). On the full 5,124-model benchmark, DFBScanner-M gets 97.22% TPR / 4.19% FPR at ~1.05 ms/model. Against the fast data-free backdoor attack DFBA (injects a backdoor in ~10 ms), DFBScanner detects it in ~0.22-0.25 ms with 100% TPR/0% FPR on ResNet18 and VGG16, versus DQ (mixed results) and MM-BD (near-total failure, 0-1 TPR out of 10-22, taking ~29-59s). Against bit-flip attacks (TBT/HPT), DFBScanner reaches 20/20 TPR with 0/10 FPR on both ResNet18 and VGG16 in 0.22 ms, versus MM-BD (7-10/20 TPR) and DQ (11-13/20 TPR). Critically, the two adaptive attacks the authors themselves designed against DFBScanner substantially degrade it: parameter regularization during training drops TPR to 65.0% (CIFAR-10) and 56.7% (GTSRB); freezing the final layer's parameters before backdoor injection (retraining only the feature extractor) drops TPR to just 11.7% (CIFAR-10, at cost of -2.73% BA/-9.38% ASR) and 2.5% (GTSRB, -2.25% BA/-7.92% ASR) -- i.e., an attacker aware of DFBScanner's final-layer-only inspection can largely evade it, at some cost to attack/benign performance.
core_contribution: The paper proposes DFBScanner, a data-free, static-parameter-only backdoor detector for DNN image classifiers. Instead of reverse-engineering trigger patterns (as in Neural Cleanse, FeatureRE, DF-TND, FreeEagle, MM-BD, BARBIE), DFBScanner inspects only the model's final (fully-connected) classification layer parameters (weight matrix W and bias vector B). It defines 12 'major' anomaly indicators over these parameters (statistical: weight mean, absolute weight mean, variance, std-dev; geometric: L1 norm, L2 norm, weight energy; bias-related: bias, weight+bias sum, normalized weight+bias sum; advanced: weight similarity/cosine distance to other classes, and a 'weight certainty' indicator derived from epistemic uncertainty), each further expanded with 4 extended metrics (z-score, normalized absolute difference, IQR upper/lower bounds), yielding 62 total indicators plus one combined IWM/IB z-score indicator. A subset of indicators ('Trojan clue matrix' IN) is selected per configuration (overall/dataset/architecture level, denoted DFBScanner-A/U/D/M) using unsupervised/supervised feature-selection methods (Isolation Forest, Mutual Information, L1-regularized logistic regression, Recursive Feature Elimination) run via the PyOD toolbox. At inference, DFBScanner computes an anomaly score vector per class, compares its cosine similarity to a pre-computed average clean-model score distribution against a threshold lambda to flag a model as backdoored, and (if flagged) identifies the class with the highest anomaly score as the backdoor target.
threat_model: Two scenarios are considered: (1) offline/training-time injection, where the attacker poisons the training data or manipulates the training pipeline and then distributes the trained model (e.g., via a model-sharing platform); (2) online/runtime injection, where the attacker modifies deployed model parameters at inference time via bit-flip methods (e.g., rowhammer-based attacks like TBT and HPT), which the paper notes tend to target the last few layers. The defender is assumed to have white-box access to the model architecture and to the final layer's parameters only (e.g., as provided by a model-sharing platform or model-infrastructure manager) but has no access to training or test data, no GPU requirement, and does not run model inference or collect any input images -- detection is purely a function of the stored final-layer weights and biases.
limitations: The paper explicitly states two limitations in its Discussion section: (1) an adversary with full knowledge of DFBScanner can evade it by freezing the final-layer parameters during backdoor injection (confirmed by their own Table X results showing TPR collapsing to 11.7%/2.5%), which the authors say would require moving parameter inspection to upper (earlier) layers -- left as future work; (2) DFBScanner only detects backdoors, it does not address mitigation/removal of a found backdoor -- the authors sketch a possible SVD-based mitigation direction (referencing prior orthogonality findings) but do not implement or evaluate it. Additional limitations evident from the results themselves: detection accuracy varies substantially by architecture and label-space size -- CIFAR-10 (10 classes) yields lower per-indicator accuracy (max 67.3%) than GTSRB (94.3%) or TinyImageNet (89.3%), attributed to CIFAR-10's smaller label space constraining final-layer capacity; VGG16 and CNN6 show comparatively low per-architecture TPR on CIFAR-10 (80.0% and 66.0% respectively in Table IV) due to what the authors call 'anomaly dilution across multiple FC layers.' The indicator-selection procedure (DFBScanner-D/M) requires a labeled configuration set of clean and backdoor models per dataset/architecture to tune the clue matrix IN and threshold lambda, which is itself a form of prior knowledge/calibration data, though the 'practical scenario' ablation (Table VIII) shows a fallback z-score-based approach works reasonably without this when scanning a mixed model pool. BARBIE, one of the strongest accuracy baselines (87.85% TPR), could only be evaluated as a binary detector since it cannot identify the backdoor target class, unlike DFBScanner.

quantitative_results: DFBScanner-M (small benchmark, 1200 backdoor/210 clean): 97.17% TPR, 0.95% FPR, ~1.06 ms/model average detection time
DFBScanner-M (full benchmark, 5124 backdoor/382 clean): 97.22% TPR, 4.19% FPR, ~1.05 ms/model
DFBScanner-A (all 62 indicators, small benchmark): 95.92% TPR, 4.23% FPR, 728.1 ms/model
DFBScanner-U (55 selected indicators): 96.25% TPR, 2.86% FPR, 719.8 ms/model
Against DFBA fast backdoor attack (~10 ms injection): DFBScanner 10/10 and 22/22 TPR with 0 FPR on ResNet18/VGG16, ~0.22-0.25 ms detection time
Against bit-flip attacks (TBT/HPT): DFBScanner 20/20 TPR, 0/10 FPR on ResNet18 and VGG16, 0.22 ms
Adaptive attack via parameter regularization: TPR drops to 65.0% (CIFAR-10, delta BA -1.44%, delta ASR -2.74%) and 56.7% (GTSRB, delta BA -1.24%, delta ASR -2.19%)
Adaptive attack via freezing final-layer parameters: TPR drops to 11.7% (CIFAR-10, delta BA -2.73%, delta ASR -9.38%) and 2.5% (GTSRB, delta BA -2.25%, delta ASR -7.92%)
Raspberry Pi 5b deployment: average detection time of 4.84 ms
Per-indicator accuracy varies by dataset: CIFAR-10 max 67.3%, GTSRB 94.3%, TinyImageNet 89.3%
Per-architecture TPR on CIFAR-10 (Table IV): ResNet18 100%, GoogLeNet 100%, PreactResNet18 99.0%, EfficientNet-B3 96.0%, VGG16 80.0%, CNN6 66.0%
baselines_compared: NC (Neural Cleanse) - 45.33% TPR / 13.81% FPR total, requires training data, up to >24h on TinyImageNet
FeatureRE - 43.67% TPR / 12.86% FPR total, requires training data, 13,402s total
DQ - 64.08% TPR / 11.43% FPR total, 0.23 ms (fastest prior method but unstable across architectures)
BARBIE - 87.85% TPR / 59.05% FPR total (high FPR), 6,107.7s total, binary-only (cannot identify target class)
